commentBody:
Major applause. Like you, I was stunned to realize that all 15 theme entries also read as actual words. No nonsense fillers clogging the grid just to make the them work.

Bravo. A great Thursday offering.
Yes! Didn’t take forever, was more than my average and I enjoyed figuring it out. A Thursday worthy of being on Thursday. Thank you to the constructor and the editor. Good night!🌙

The following xwstats.com numbers are very early stats, but still:

🌎  Global Stats
Difficulty
Very Hard
Median Solve Time
15:10
Median Solver
55% slower
⚡0% of users solved faster than their Thursday average.
0% solved much faster (>20%) than their Thursday average.
🐢100% of users solved slower than their Thursday average.
100% solved much slower (>20%) than their Thursday average.

I think those stats are based on fewer than usual solves for the first 25 minutes of solving, which in and of itself is telling.

I don't think the difficulty level is going to change much, at all.

Myself, I got more stuck than usual, with quite a lot open on the top and bottom left.  The trick didn't hold me up that much; it was the dense cluing reminiscent of the olden days.

There's not going to be much "too easy" griping today.
Great fun! I caught on reasonably early (for a Thursday) but still took longer than my average time. It was great to gradually work it out.
Indic? 🙄

Jus barely bea my average time. I go stuck on the top lef corner for too long.

Grea theme and a solid Thursday puzzle.
Oh right CHARGE! As in that!!! Oh good lord.  Someone take away by bar card.

I'm all in favor of the many forms of wordplay that Will Shortz has introduced to the NYT crosswords.

But if I had my druthers I'd be totally in favor of going back to a time when no brand names were ever mentioned, people's sexual orientations were never alluded to, there was no slang that only a niche crowd knows, and no answers involved not-nice words (the kind that people used to teach their young children not to say).
Too easy! Monday anyone? Fun nonetheless
I'm one of those who occasionally wonders why people complain so much entering rebuses, but today was my turn to join the ranks.

Once again I barely squeaked by.

I saw that we several answers were going to be words ending in T fairly early, but like Steve L (I always like it when my experience and Steve L's are similar) I found a lot of the answers hard to come by.

For example it took me quite a while to see INDIC(T), LEISURE, SCRIBES, didn't know ADP and TESS.

PINEY seemed pretty difficult to me, as did GIGABIT, because I've only ever heard of GIGABYTE.  

I like APOLOGY for [Fault line?], but again, that was anything but a Monday clue, and I'd say more like a Friday or Saturday.

But I had a good time with it.  A little confusion and panic before a gentle thought followed by everything falling into place is a nice way to spend some time.

I solved it and was left stunned that I didn't hear the chimes. Stared at it. Then finally decided to try it without having the extra t added on the Rebus. I wish it would have accepted the rebus answers, though I do now see that the Ts were there in the graph of the puzzle.

I wish there was Wordplay for the Midi puzzle.
I don’t get today’s theme.
Help?
That was a tough solve. 30 minutes, but the vast majority of that time was spent in the NW.
Awesome! It's not often that I can figure out the theme AND complete it without looking at the column. Thanks for a fun Thursday!
I thought this one was crazy fun. Because I read this forum, I knew going in that a Thursday would have a twisT. Loved it.
Great theme - my favorite Thursday/Sunday in a while! Kudos to the author
This is identical to a Thursday puzzle that was published in 2019(?) does anyone remember what I’m talking about? Getting serious deja vu!
A tough Thursday that makes me feel like I’m getting better at crosswords. This would have sunk me a year ago. 71 day streak stays alive

I’m down with a vertical theme and the clever trick suits me to a T. 

So very elegant that the T-less entries are valid words. 

Great work, Rafa and Sala. Flawless execution.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: Loved it.  Finally...an actual Thursday after so long without.  And then I realized it's a 2020 retread.

"You said there'd be Earl Grey but there isn't any! You're such a teas!"

("Sorry, how brewed of me!")

Thought this was a grand Thursday. 

One problem the app though: The main screen shows (correctly) that I've got a 424 day streak and all puzzles this week are gold. But when I go into my stats it tells me I'm only on a 1 day streak and my longest streak ended on Tuesday. If I bring up the web version, Wednesday shows up blue. Any ideas?

If I was a pop diva and had won three of the four major awards given to performers -- an Emmy, Grammy and Obie -- my ego would feel like it was lacking something.

Fabulous puzzle. Top Tier. Titillating. Too good. To come up with all those words or phrases that make sense with or without the final tea, to place them symmetrically, to fill in the whole grid with interesting and witty clues and fill, what a Triumph. Loved the NOSEEUM /UM NO combo, the piney gin sharing space with a sommelier's fine palate, the obscure clue for Tess (at least to me) and equally obscure -- to me -- Ottessa. 

Yesterday the lake was SERENE. Today it is RIPPLING. I have a bad feeling about this lake. 

Plus, who doesn't like a puzzle where a tyrant with a bent ego, known for declaring whatever the scribes write about him to be fake and demanding they erase the truth, gets a stern talking-to from a pop diva, who takes him to the lav, makes him get down on his knees, and gives him a big whack on his backside with something piney and roughly the weight and size of an atlas, a wallop you might see at an MMA event while in the company of the Secret ari of State, who should be negotiating how to open sea lanes again but instead has taken a ripsaw to any diplomatic options and has to cede to the crazy demands of the leisure-loving tyrant; yes, whacks him hard enough that he actually does cry out an apology!

I love it when I think I've figured a theme out, then even after completing the grid, I realise that there is some much more to it than I thought.

I figured out the revealer and the missing T's, but I was not until I came to this page and read other comments that I realised two more things: first that all the T-less words were still actual words, and more importantly that they all ended on the massive dark squared Ts (that I somehow failed to notice!). 

Mind blown. Really well done.

ADP and TESS were unknowns so the bottom left used up a lot of time. 

The rest was elegant and very much Thursday level for me. The trick and the double use came relatively quickly and brought a smile

Thanks

It’s T-time in the Times, therefore time for tea. 

Terrific Thursday!

I enjoyed the theme, especially after noticing the big black T s in the grid...
No See Ums: I think that's been in several puzzles, and I have been bitten on the nose.  Wasn't too hard, did not include oreo,  about half my avg time.
Good Thursday.

I had some hard times on the western side of the grid. My biggest problem? I had always heard the expression as 'done to a T' in the context of cooking. 'Down to a T' had never crossed my path (though I don't see any complaints about it here). Meanwhile, I was twisting myself into a conga line trying to find words in the southwest block to fit DONE. A brief online search hasn't turned up anything definitive. Can anyone explain which, if either, version is correct?

I saw Rafael Musa’s byline and immediately hoped we would finally get a challenging, tricky puzzle that was worthy of a Thursday.
I was not disappointed! Oddly I got there a little sideways. I read the first clue as away from instead of towards and tried to fit AORTAE in; then put the E in the black square and used it for E-VENT—which still left real words in the grid. That primed me for the rest of the puzzle and I quickly saw the Ts and TEASED out the rest of the theme (and fixed the top).
But even once I got the theme it was fun and challenging. Amazing dense construction to get 15 clues that still left real words with the T off ( a little leeway for INDIC and ABU), stacked, and the perfect revealer at the bottom. And some very clever ones at that—DAYSPAS, OPENSEA.
And then, finally, the editors didn’t overcompensate by making the rest of the clues gimmes or too easy. THANK YOU!!! Fun clues for LEISURE, POPDIVA, APOLOGY and TAROT.

As mentioned something similar has been done before but this had its own vibe and was by no means derivative. Bravo and Brava to the constructors!!!

I looked at the grid before I began, and I saw all the T's, but forgot about them while I was solving. There were so many words that were missing a final T that I just thought it was some kind of rebus ruse.  Of course, when I finished I had a mega-DOH moment.

Rafael and Sala, it was indeed a TEEEremendous T party, and I loved it.  Thank you!

This was a fun puzzle. 
But because I’m a dope, I made all 15 theme cells, a 2 letter rebus. (xT). 

When I finished the grid, I didn’t get a gold. I reviewed, and didn’t think there were any errors. 
I removed all the 2 letter rebus, removing the Ts. Gold Star. 

I came to say this was bad programming. 
I now realize the Ts were proved by the grid. 

I’m an IDIO.

Almost had it but the SW had me stuck. I had DIREC instead of DIVER and it just wasn’t working. Gave up and read the column. I had all the “hard” answers, and TOAT, but was missing DOWN. That helped me finish the SW corner. 

As a relative beginner, not too bad. Maybe if I had slept on it I would have solved it on my own.
I don't like a vulgar term for rear in my puzzle. Keep it civiliized.
I don’t understand “popdiva” as someone known for her runs?
Oof.

I started at the bottom, so ended up being easier than expected. Still tricky in parts. It also didn't count as a solve for me unfortunately, because I put the ts in as a rebus 😩. Would have been a worthy Thursday if the ts were randomly dispersed. All in all, a good puzzle. But I'm still waiting for a great one.
Ha! A fun one, and not too tricky. Left a mark on my statistics, though, when I dozed off and ended up quadrupling my Wednesday average :-/
Fantastic Thursday puzzle. Just what I was looking for. Well done
Thursday is my favorite puzzle day, because of puzzles like this one! Thank you!

When I printed out my puzzle, the first thing I noticed was the giant T's. Then somehow I promptly forgot they existed, and until I came to the revealer I had a T's written in the grey boxes to keep track and wondered how the missing letters would be explained. Didn't see the forest for the trees. This was an entertaining solve for me. In recent Thursday puzzles, once you saw the trick, it was game over. This one had interesting clues that kept the puzzle alive. I thought for sure [Source of a deal with The Devil?] was going to be a reference to Robert Johnson. https://youtu.be/Yd60nI4sa9A?si=YbOi107I4ozae7Uu

HIP HIP HURRUMBA!!!

This is how you do Thursday.

Very much Enjoyed this Thursday. After a few of the down answers were missing the final T, I imagined them in the black squares. It wasn't until I got to the revealer, I then saw the Forest through the 'Tees'! Oh, and thanks for reminding me that noseeum (?no see em?) and black fly season is about to begin here in Maine!🪰🪰🪰
Hi Sala! Would that celebration be tonight (Thursday), or was it last night (Wednseday)? What time, appx.? I could stop by.
I found that pleasingly challenging today after some very easy ones last week. This took twice as long as my usual Thursday.
Don't know if it was an Easter egg or a terrific coincidence, but I love the homophonic perfect theme echo in TEA SET.

This weeks has just been made unpleasant by the amount of proper name trivia and frequent natick.

Not sure if it's random or part of some meta theme but IMO it's much worse.

Do difficulty without relying on knowing the names of payroll services etc.

A nice Thursday puzzle that had something for almost everyone: an aesthetically pleasing grid; a gentle twist that was integral to the solve; and some clever clueing. I appreciated fault line for APOLOGY. I also liked NOSEEUMs, which are best encountered in a puzzle rather than in real life.

Great puzzle. Just missed my average by a couple of minutes, but there were times I was wondering if I would finish it at all.

I got the trick easily enough, but there were enough words that were obscure to me that it was all skipping around, finishing a section at a time, until it finally fell into place.

Thanks!
fanTasTic!!!!!!!!

Tremendous.

Thinking this touts top-tier talent!

Themed Thursday thrill!

Thank you!
Incredible design and fun, challenging puzzle. What I didn’t understand was INDIC.  All the others doubled as words but is that referring to Indic languages?
Am I the only one who thought that 57D ("Modern intensifying suffix" was "ASF" -- and then thought, "Wow, Will is really pushing the edge here!"?
Loved this puzzle! It should have been easier (knowing the 15 down answers had to be words both with and without a t), but it wasn't for me. I Enjoyed the challenge very much, though.

I got greedy and plunged into the solve rather than scanning the empty grid for hints about what the puzzle was about. As a result, it took me longer to crack the riddle.  

To play on a well-used expression, I couldn’t see the gimmick for the tees! No-knows and tricky cluing compounded my confusion.

The longer my bafflement, the higher the tension, so that when the curtain finally fell, the aha was huge – epic! – bolstered by a thrilling whoosh to the finish.

That was aha number one.  Number two came when I saw that the theme answers all were genuine words without the black-square Ts – there my jaw dropped due to the skill and talent behind the construction.

Dessert was a lovely serendipity -- that there's a down DOWN and an up UP.

A worducken! -- An aha bogo amidst a brain pleaser housed in a top-notch build.  Result?  A strong Puzzle of the Year Thursday candidate.  This was royally good.  Thank you, Sala and Rafael!
TEASET indeed!
Nearly gave up on the NW corner, yuck. But every once in a while I'm amazed at how a constructor manages to pull an idea off. Definitely one of the best of the year so far.

Saw the large T's and guessed the theme instantly ... but solving still took almost 20 minutes. So the level of difficulty seemed about right for a Thursday.

Excellent theme, expertly executed. Especially liked that each vertical entry that extended DOWN TOAT was valid crossword fill in its own right. DAYSPAS(T) and OPENSEA(T) were especially good.

To me, a puzzle of the year contender for sure. Bravo!
Picked up on the theme early but the arcane fill took me a while to get

Typical tough Thursday workout, but a very enjoyable challenge. Finally tumbling to the trick was the huge turning point and that's always a nice touch. Two thumbs up. And... a rather appropriate puzzle find today. A Thursday from January 13, 2005 by Robert Zimmerman. The reveal in that one (right in the middle of the puzzle) was: "Key to answering 16- and 58- across, and 10- and 27-down." TFORTWO And those answers: TABLEFORT TSCOMPANY TERRIBLETS TBITSWORTH Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/13/2005&g=36&d=A ....

I almost never notice grid art, but this time I did, and it sure helped. The NW corner was very sticky, but somehow I figured out the theme very quickly. And that's why for me, this puzzle was fairly easy. But the construction was a marvel!

I think BRAT and OPENSEAT were my first theme answers, and I worked my way down then across and up, until I finally was able to complete that pesky NW corner. 

I'm not sure the term NOSEEUMs was ever used on the east coast, and though I've been here in Vegas for 20 years, we didn't really have mosquitos until just a few years ago, when the incessant building (i.e. more swimming pools and other water-hogging features) brought that term into popular usage. I never heard of it until then.

Sean McGowan,
Re: your thoughts on 22A (in Trick Clues)
Need we remind our new columnist that a solver not knowing an answer is not a puzzle construction fault? Even in jest?
Nice to finally have a Thursday puzzle on Thursday (or any day). Thanks, Rafa and Sala (two To a T).
Agree with Mr. McGowan - ten out of ten.  No notes.

I enjoyed this puzzle.  It was a real challenge, and I worried periodically that I wouldn't be able to solve it on my own.  That's what I like in a puzzle.

But the theme?  I can't say I was terribly impressed.  I assume everyone saw all those T's in the grid, and, this being Thursday, I quickly figured out their significance.

Since getting DOWN in the SW came very late in my solve, it took me a while to figure out which words ABUtting the T's would be themers, so that added another nice degree of difficulty.
And it was exceptionally clever that all 15(!!!) themers worked well without their final T's.  Perhaps this was the most impressive feature of the gimmick for me.

But what I enjoyed most was the general difficulty of the puzzle as a whole.  So many unknowns and plenty of clever misdirects.
Among the ones that haven't been mentioned much, I most enjoyed [This isn't working!].  It took me a long time to notice there weren't any quotation marks around it and that I should be focusing on the "This".

Fine puzzle overall.
Friday level of difficulty with a sufficiently clever Thursday gimmick.
Thanks, Rafael Musa and Sala Wanetick!

Remember in the Disney movie when Aladdin's pet monkey swiped the ruby in the Cave of Wonders and ended up destroying the cave?

Peak Abu!

The best Thursday we've had in a long time. I finished the puzzle and got halfway through the Wordplay column before it hit me that the DOWN TO A T answers all made sense without their Ts. Absolutely amazing.

I'm always excited to see Rafael Musa's name in the byline, and this puzzle did not disappoint!

Loved the big, graphic theme of this puzzle, but I’m confused by a couple non-theme entries: 51 Across is clued as [Home security co.] and solves to ADP (not ADT). While 60 Across is clued as [Big payroll service co.] and solves not to ADP (a big payroll service company), but to ETAS, a German automotive design firm. Help?

Great Thursday although it took me forever.  Got the theme early, or so I thought, with PERCOCE in 11A early on.  Top left was blank until the end as a result.  Tried a bunch of different things before “Down” to a T finally clicked.  Bravo!

Really impressive puzzle! I didn't know NOSEEUM but was able to squeeze it out of the grid. I greatly appreciated how some of those T clues weren't too tough so that catching on to the gimmick didn't need the revealer.
Triple T in a crossword 🥀
I'm usually not a fan of Thursday puzzles but this one was great. Loved the offbeat cluing.

One more puzzle find. A Tuesday from April 15, 2014 by Gary Cee. First - four theme answers, straightforwardly clued: "Grounds for impeachment" HIGHTREASON "TV show anchored by Bill O'Reilly" INSIDEEDITION "Underhanded attack." LOWBLOW "Hundred-to-one odds, say." OUTSIDECHANCE And then... "Batter's reward after pitches like those described at the starts of 17-, 24-, 39 and 51-across." BASEONBALLS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/15/2014&g=51&d=A ....
I had the same route of discovery as the commentator (who I also thank for explaining the 'no-see-um' answer, which I didn't get). And I had the same aha moments. Loved this puzzle.
mistake in puzzle... you left the t off the answer for venus.. the rock is basalt...  no such rock as basal

Very fun puzzle! Parsed the gimmick fairly quickly but it still took me 50% longer than my average, thanks to not knowing my home security companies and the fact that RIPSAW and “rib saw“ are both legitimate answers to 43D. Still, a rio(t). Well done!

I did not have much my first run through the Acrosses, though i did have PBS and ISAAC, and I took note of the Themer clue.  Then as I was going along with the Downs I got to the one with the Tot, saw the B _ A and thought that should be BRAT and suddenly noticed the Ts in the grid. Ah ha! And so it went on from there. 

Had a tough time one the west side, but as I look at the finished puzzle, I don't know why.

Nice job, Sala and Rafael.

I arrived at NOSE-EUM and just thought EUM was another crossword word for an insect, the small one infecting people’s noses in antiquity or something.   The U was my last entry and the music surprised me.

In my learning curve over the past year guessing likely word fragments is one skill that regular solving has developed.  The most obvious case is the “S” at the end of a plural.

Anyone else see the face of a vampire on today’s layout?  Top T is the widow’s peak, bottom two are fangs, then you have the nose, eyes, and ears.  They ROVEd and ATEUP fluid from ATRIA.

I did some flailing around even before getting to the Themers.  
The "Facilities"...ha ha!  WCS, right?  Eventually, LOO, yes?

Abandoning that dead end, I went for PBS...and then the Kid who might be grounded...starting with a B...I was led to think of the proportion of BOY students landed in my Special Ed classes (mostly due to the kind of behavior that gets kids grounded), But the Y in the middle of 35A looked pretty unlikely.   BRA? Oh, come now.  
 Finally, it was Whoopie and Rita who helped me see past the EGO to the EGOT, and all was sunny and bright thereafter.  

Last thing into the grid was the second P in the 1D entry.  
POP DIVA has runs?  In her stockings?  Makes no sense to me, but I vaguely remembered that X is some big deal thing, so....
Voilá
!

"whatever your mind sees in the Rorschach test that is an unfilled grid"
You mean a swastika?
Got off to a rough star, but soon the end was in sigh

This was a "keep the faith" puzzle for me as I scampered away from the fiendish NW and hoped I'd be able to solve it when I got back to it.  Alas, my faith was not rewarded and I ended up having to cheat on TAO.  

The theme was clever and baffling until I saw it at BRA/BRAT.  I was so eager to write in TO A T as the revaler that I failed to see it was a two-part answer and wrote it in at 63A instead of 65A.  What was that first word ending in N because of SSN?  Aha -- DOWN!  But I needed crosses to get it.  DOWN TO A T.  Yes, that is indeed a phrase.

Great clue for LEISURE.

Thought for the day about 18A:  While I like my cleaning products completely odorless rather than PINEY, I understand that some people do like them PINEY.  But please keep PINEY out of my gin!!  Thanks so much!

A puzzle where I was grateful for the fact that the revealer eliminated much angst.  It was nice to know that the missing "T"s were only in the Downs and not, well, everywhere.  And for once in my life, I really enjoyed the grid art.  The design took nothing away from the cleverness of the puzzle, while adding a certain je ne sais quoi that was funny and delightful.  A great job of construction and a real treat for the solver!
Testing testing 123
Is anyone else having the problem that View All Replies won't open up to the rest of the replies? Or is that just a me problem?

Despite the glaring hints I also struggled at first so went for the reveal and confidently plugged in DOne TOAT.  Sat with that even after I finally grokked what was going on.  That was my last little mess to clean up.  Very clever, but . . .

. . . PINEY?   Not in our house!

Many thanks.
Definitely not in my lane. Even for a Thursday.
I did. I did start using ESC to fill. To be fair, I started on the Downs. Hah! Nice start to the day, with a fat red handprint on my forehead. Thanks for the fun one!
Great puzzle. Fun theme and some challenging and clever cluing. Perfect for a Thursday outing.

It's interesting to see how many people, including our columnist @Sean McGowan, had never heard of NO-SEE-UMs before.

Growing up on Long Island, I had never heard of them either.  I first came across the beastie when I went to college, from an outdoorsy friend who grew up in the Mid-West.  I remember thinking that it was a regional bug with a cute regional name.

There not much spoken of here in the Bluegrass, and so I'm wondering again whether the bug and/or its name is regional.
Anyone with knowledge of the matter?
It took me a wander to get going. But it was smooth sailing once I got the theme. I really appreciated that the truncated words were, well, words. No shortcuts, no excuses !

Weird that using the rebut feature to complete certain clues came up as wrong.... I felt like I solved it, but then had all these wrong answers until I removed the additional letters. Disappointing because I thought the two word duality was very satisfying.

I don't get it. First I did the puzzle with rebuses. That didn't work. Then I removed all the Ts and that didn't work. Upon checking the puzzle, I made no mistakes.  All words were correct. I have no idea what happened. Could it be a techniical flaw?

Its been a long time, for a host of reasons--mostly vocational--since I came to wordplay to test out other reaction to this particular puzzle. It is not the most difficult. The hardest solve for me was X since i dont twitter or whatever it is called now. It was the wonderful play on the T-formations with the dual completeness of the "down to a t" solutions. A shame with the classic T formations the only football reference was a pro set quarterback. This is my favorite solve since the outside the box puzzle a few years back

Fun puzzle - and just tricky enough for a Thursday!  Loved the added trick that all the theme answers are also words without the final "T" - except I don't know what INDIC and ABU are... can someone help?

I had practically nothing on first pass, and even pulled up Wikipedia in preparation to do some "learning". Glad I didn't quit so fast. Got a toehold in the lower third, and AHA - the revealer. Excellent Thursday!

Even though I got the gimmick pretty early, I still flailed terribly on this one.  It didn’t help that I treated the black bar on the middle right side as a T (UMNO/UMNOT) for a long time.  Finally I finished about 15 minutes longer than my average.

I didn't see the grid art, and didn't catch on until I got to DELIST, because I'm a stock market guy. I almost got it with TEA SET, BTW, because I  have one of my grandmother's. One question: is INDIC doper slang? Something I might see on a label in a dispensary? Great Thursday gimmick.
Horrible puzzle
Re: 1D in the midi. I have been sending emails for 30 years and have never heard a "woosh" when I click send. I'm guessing this must be an Apple thing?
That was a bear. I had AORTI (which isn't actually word, I don't think?) in the top left for a loooong time to much consternation.

I caught on very early, since I always have my guard up on Thurs. And it was obvious to me that there were a bunch of Ts in the grid from the outset. But the thing that tripped me up was that I put down INDUCT instead of INDICT, because I was thinking of induction battery chargers! I also had AM I HOT instead of AM I NOT, since that made more sense to me. So the cross was PUHEY which I figured was some word I didn’t know. I spent 20 minutes checking everything in the puzzle until I finally realized my error. I liked this puzzle!
Question:  Is it a crossword rule or convention that a rebus is only acceptable if the correct word is formed?  Is it unacceptable for the correct word to be embedded in the rebus?

I had exactly the same path, using the northeast quadrant and trying rebuses first before realizing the down entries above the Ts ended in T and then I was “off to the races.” Very satisfying and solved 28 minutes faster than my average Thursday. But I failed to see that the entries were also good words without the Ts at the ends. Glad I came to read this article and increase my awe.

Aha!  I just figured out why my completely correct puzzle did not give me a win. After I removed all the rebuses, leaving all words correct, I did not hit refresh on my laptop. So the corrected puzzle did not immediately register with the system. I lost because of electronic slowness.

I really liked the theme today, although it was a bit easy to spot.  I love that all the theme answers were valid words without the T.  As soon as I got to OPEN SEA(T) it was clear how it worked.  Some of them like ABU(T) were still tricky.

I did feel some of the fill didn’t work.  Proper nouns in tricky areas like OTTESSA and TAO.  And answers like PAINMED which felt like too general an answer for such a specific clue.  And RIPSAW gave me a ton of trouble cause it crossed other tough clues and I hadn’t heard of that tool.

When I saw the clue for “The word between here and there” I tried AND first cause I thought the clue was being cute.

I. Cant. Even....... These two constructors have assembled what may be the finest puzzle I've ever seen. The first trick, words ending without their T, wasn't good enough for these two. Nooo, they shot for the moon and made the (otherwise "unclued)") words make sense too. Early, I know, but easily PotY material here. Terrific job you two 👍👍👍

I got through this well but no music. Spent a long time trying to find it.  Opened iPhone and list mode to look at each word. There I was able to see the ] character where I should have been.  Very hard to see on the Mac as the lower part of the character coincided with the box.  How I typed ] I will never know.
A rebus is a rebus is a rebus. Tiresome and tedious.
Loved this one! The “unclued” words were a particularly elegant touch. Kudos to the constructors!

I don't mind a rebus, but I wish it had accepted all the letters with the extra Ts, well, with the extra Ts. Knowing what I do about rebus puzzles my last resort is always "only one letter per square" and that did work, but it's extra tedious work and it's weird it wouldn't be accepted in the full version.

The rebus themeitself was cute though. I like when it makes no sense to start with but comes together, like this one did.
Is 49 across supposed to be a nod to todays Strands, or is it just a coincidence?
I had to resort to looking up the P in ADP/RIPSAW, but at least that's finally i. I'm bea.

Oh, the relief after a good workout is complete. A little extra time today and I never shy away from a lookup or two on Thursday to make sure I’m on the right track. This was suhwee(T)! 
Thank you, thank you!!!
Hard to believe there are people who have never encountered a swarm of no-see-ums.  They are memorable.

I really wish the columnists and occasionally, commenters, would stop referring to tricks like today’s—which resemble the traditional, Concentration-style picture rebuses with the plus and minus signs—as rebuses. In a crossword context, “rebus” has always (since 1954) referred to symbols, numerals, punctuation, or multiple letters in white squares, rather than the usual single letters. Calling other things “rebuses” is confusing a lot of people.

Nitpick: While often rip saws have coarse teeth, they don’t need to; there are fine-toothed rip saws. What makes a saw a rip saw is the geometry of the teeth—rip saws are filed to cut along the grain, not across it. Dovetail saws, for instance, are filed as rip saws, but have very fine teeth due to the precision required to cut such fine joinery.

@Francis for some reason, Internet speeds are always given in “bits” not “bytes”. Maybe because it results in a number 8 times as large. Usually given (these days) in Mbps (megabits per second). Gigabit refers to throughput of 1000 Mbps (1 Gbps). If referring to bytes, you would generally capitalize the B.

@Jeb Jones it’s actually because at the lowest levels of the networking “stack,” the transmission doesn’t necessarily have to be parsed out as “bytes,” even though in modern networking, I think it always is.

@Jeb Jones Because a byte's size is meaningful in encoding letters. A bit size is all that's needed for some simple data, so it makes more sense to use gigabits for communications speeds but gigabytes for how much data can be stored on a drive.

@Brian Coffey
Former pro carpenter here; the coarse (offset) teeth of the RIP SAW help to keep your cut from drifting, while the fine toothed blade of a crosscut saw has a tendency to follow the grain of the wood. I only ever rip on a table saw, as ripping with a hand saw is quite tedious,
@Brian Coffey I initially had BOWSAW which has very course teeth as well.

@Grant, that’s incorrect. What makes a saw a rip saw is that the teeth are only sharpened across the front. They work like mini chisels chopping into the grain of the wood. Crosscut saws’ teeth are filed on two sides, making the shapes more like a knife so the can cut across the grain. Fine or coarse has to do with the number of teeth, and if you rip boards by hand you want fewer teeth to make quicker work of it. But again, a dovetail saw is filed as a rip saw, as are tenon saws… but neither are “coarse.”

@Brian Coffey - Although it is true that fine-toothed ripsaw blades exist, by far the majority of them are coarse toothed. What you say about the grind of the teeth is also true, but irrelevant to the clue and answer. A clue cannot possibly contain all the characteristics that define whatever the answer is. The crossword would probably have to take up half or more of the newspaper if that were so. 

This is a crossword puzzle, not a mechanical engineering treatise on saw blade design. The clue and answer are fine as written.

@Susan K

One of the keys to crossword success is the ability to fill in things you’re not interested in. It could be rap, sports, TV and movie trivia, geography, or if your an international solvers, US-based stuff like brand names and corporations (I’m looking at you, ADP). 

I don’t watch much TV, but I’m aware that the show in question is quite popular these days, and the name in question is an ordinary first name. I try to use the crosses to figure out what name it is. 

Constructors want to make puzzles puzzling, but solvable, and usually in cases like this, the crosses are helpful. 

Of course, unless you have developed the necessary solving skills, you’re going to hit some snags. But don’t be discouraged. After a while, you sense what they’re looking for.
